flashcards.front: What is the key advantage of balanced search trees over basic BSTs?
flashcards.back: Balanced search trees guarantee O(log n) time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion operations, whereas basic BSTs can degrade to O(n) when unbalanced.

flashcards.front: Define balance factor in AVL trees.
flashcards.back: Balance factor is the difference between heights of left and right subtrees: BF = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree). In AVL trees, it must be -1, 0, or +1.

flashcards.front: What are the four rotation cases in AVL trees?
flashcards.back: LL Case (left-left imbalance), RR Case (right-right imbalance), LR Case (left-right imbalance), and RL Case (right-left imbalance).

flashcards.front: What is the maximum height of an AVL tree with n nodes?
flashcards.back: The maximum height of an AVL tree is approximately 1.44 * log₂(n+2), ensuring O(log n) complexity.

flashcards.front: List the five properties of Red-Black trees.
flashcards.back: 1) Every node is red or black, 2) Root is black, 3) Every leaf (NIL) is black, 4) Red node cannot have red children, 5) Every path from root to leaf has same number of black nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum height of a Red-Black tree with n nodes?
flashcards.back: The maximum height is at most 2*log₂(n+1), guaranteeing O(log n) operations.

flashcards.front: Define B-Tree order (m) and minimum degree (t).
flashcards.back: A B-Tree of order m can have at most m children and (m-1) keys. Minimum degree t relates as m = 2t-1 (maximum keys) and at least t-1 keys (minimum).

flashcards.front: What is the minimum degree (t) of a B-Tree?
flashcards.back: Minimum degree t defines the bounds: each node except root must have at least t-1 keys (t children), and can have at most 2t-1 keys (2t children). Root has at least 1 key.

flashcards.front: What is a split operation in B-Trees?
flashcards.back: When a node becomes full (2t-1 keys), it is split into two nodes with t-1 keys each, and the middle key moves to the parent node.

flashcards.front: In AVL insertion, after inserting a node, how do you fix LL imbalance?
flashcards.back: Perform single right rotation on the imbalance node, promoting the left child to root position.

flashcards.front: In AVL insertion, how do you fix LR imbalance?
flashcards.back: Perform left rotation on left child, then right rotation on the imbalance node (double rotation).

flashcards.front: Why are rotations necessary in balanced trees?
flashcards.back: Rotations restructure the tree to maintain balance property while preserving the binary search tree order property (inorder traversal remains sorted).

flashcards.front: What is the black-height of a Red-Black tree path?
flashcards.back: Black-height is the number of black nodes (excluding NIL leaves) on any path from a given node to leaves. All paths must have equal black-height.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of searching in a B-Tree?
flashcards.back: O(logₜ n) where t is the minimum degree. Since t is constant relative to n, it's O(log n).

flashcards.front: Compare AVL trees and Red-Black trees.
flashcards.back: AVL trees are more strictly balanced (height difference ≤1), offering faster lookups but more rotations during insertion. Red-Black trees have relaxed balance, fewer rotations, better for frequent insertions.
